purpose: Answers: How do commodity shocks create systemic financial fragility through firm-level balance sheet transitions?
components: Hedge financing: income covers all obligations (stable at moderate commodity prices)
Speculative financing: income covers interest but not principal (requires refinancing; activated at elevated commodity prices)
Ponzi financing: income covers neither interest nor principal (requires asset sales or new borrowing; activated at extreme commodity prices)
Threshold transitions: firms shift categories as commodity costs cross working-capital thresholds
Selective fragility: speculative and Ponzi units fail while hedge-dominant sectors survive (2022-23 pattern)
current_assessment: Provides the microeconomic mechanism connecting commodity prices to financial stability that the KB does not currently capture. The 2022-23 selective fragility pattern is exactly what the framework predicts. Fed SLOOS data provides supporting evidence. Quantitative calibration of threshold transitions remains imprecise and firm-specific.
limitations: Quantitative calibration of hedge → speculative → Ponzi thresholds is imprecise and firm-specific
Does not specify commodity price levels that trigger transitions (these depend on firm-specific balance sheets)
SLOOS data provides supporting but not granular Minsky classification
Has not been formally integrated with the KB's passthrough coefficient framework
